refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation window. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of availability as (total_up_time / total_evaluation_time); comparison against 0.999 threshold or customer-defined target. Logging of all link transitions and root causes.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to minimize link unavailability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link status visibility to the tele-operator and site manager.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall support the required bandwidth and latency for teleoperation commands and telemetry.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual independent communication paths (primary and backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; log all transitions.
DP-B: Deploy a single high-reliability communication link with built-in error correction and retransmission; implement watchdog timers and graceful degradation.
DP-C: Use a mesh or multi-hop network topology with dynamic routing to provide path redundancy; implement link aggregation across multiple carriers.
DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining dual paths (DP-A) with link health monitoring and operator notification; include fallback to safe state if availability target is at risk.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid dual-path teleoperation architecture with continuous link health monitoring, automatic failover, operator notification, and safe-state fallback. Primary path uses dedicated network; secondary path uses alternative carrier or mesh routing. Link availability is measured in real-time and reported to Safety Services and the tele-operator.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure quality (external dependency, but manageable via SLA).
DP-D couples operator awareness to Safety Services notification mechanism (internal coupling, acceptable via SafetyAlert interface).
DP-D couples failover logic to vehicle control state machine (internal coupling, acceptable via Tasking interface).
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP is decoupled from specific hardware vendors and carrier choices, allowing flexibility. Coupling to Safety Services is intentional and required for safety criticality. No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dual independent network paths are available at the worksite (primary + secondary carrier or mesh).
Assumption: Failover latency (< 100 ms) is acceptable for safe vehicle operation.
Risk: If both paths fail simultaneously, link availability target cannot be met; mitigation requires redundancy at network provider level.
Risk: Operator response time to link loss notification may exceed safe shutdown window; mitigation requires automatic safe-state transition.
Risk: Measuring availability over a short evaluation period may not capture rare failure modes; long-term monitoring recommended.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the evaluation period duration (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year) and define 'operational time' (e.g., excludes scheduled maintenance windows). Specify failover latency requirement and safe-state behavior on dual-path failure. Confirm network infrastructure SLA with customer.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-path communication architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and operator interface for link status. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking) to command equipment and receive telemetry, and with Safety Services (via SafetyAlert) to report link state and trigger safe-state transitions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional command-and-telemetry link. Teleoperation sends operator commands to Vehicle; Vehicle sends telemetry (position, state, sensor data) back to Teleoperation. Failover must preserve Tasking continuity.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation reports link state changes (loss, recovery) and availability metrics to Safety Services. Safety Services uses this to trigger safe-state transitions or operator notifications.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation reports link availability metrics, failover events, and network diagnostics to monitoring/logging systems (Site Manager dashboard, maintenance logs).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation depends on external network infrastructure (primary and secondary carriers or mesh). This is a dependency, not an owned interface; SLA must be negotiated.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover, and operator notification. Dual-path management, health monitoring, and failover state machine are core to this module. Minor diffusion: Vehicle module must implement safe-state transition logic (owned by Vehicle, triggered by SafetyAlert from Teleoperation).

Safety Services could own link availability monitoring and safe-state triggering, but this would diffuse teleoperation-specific logic (dual-path failover, operator notification) across modules. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle module owns equipment control and safe-state logic, but should not own network communication architecture. Assigning link availability to Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle control and network infrastructure, reducing modularity.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container, but assigning link availability here would bypass the Teleoperation module's responsibility. Not appropriate for detailed implementation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of CR-NGHE-010 because it encapsulates the dual-path communication architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and operator interface. This keeps network-specific concerns isolated from vehicle control logic and safety state machines. Coupling to Safety Services (via SafetyAlert) is intentional and required for safe-state transitions.
containment_risks: Risk: If network infrastructure is not available or SLA is not met, Teleoperation cannot achieve the availability target. Mitigation: Negotiate network SLA with customer and document as a constraint.

Risk: Failover latency may exceed safe shutdown window if Vehicle module is slow to respond to SafetyAlert. Mitigation: Define and test failover latency requirements; implement automatic safe-state transition in Vehicle if link loss is detected.
Risk: Operator may not respond to link loss notification in time. Mitigation: Implement automatic safe-state transition; do not rely solely on operator awareness.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has authority to implement dual-path failover and that Vehicle module will implement automatic safe-state transition on SafetyAlert. Define the interface contract for SafetyAlert (e.g., link loss triggers safe-state within [X] ms). Specify network infrastructure requirements and SLA.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (hybrid dual-path architecture with failover and monitoring) directly addresses the FR (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and implementable. No functional gaps detected. The DP includes redundancy, health monitoring, and safe-state fallback, which are necessary to meet the high safety criticality of teleoperation.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Vehicle module must implement safe-state transition logic in response to SafetyAlert. This is acceptable because Vehicle owns the safe-state definition; Teleoperation only triggers it.
Minor diffusion: Network infrastructure (primary and secondary carriers) is external to NGHE Solution. This is a dependency, not diffusion; must be managed via SLA.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module cleanly contains the dual-path failover logic, link health monitoring, and operator notification. The module boundary is clear: Teleoperation owns communication architecture; Vehicle owns equipment control; Safety Services owns safe-state policy. No significant diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: continuous monitoring of link state during evaluation period; calculation of availability metric; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Logging of all link transitions and failover events supports root cause analysis.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≥0.999 link availability. Success criteria are measurable (availability metric ≥ 0.999). Ownership is unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Clarify evaluation period duration and definition of 'operational time' (e.g., excludes scheduled maintenance).
Define failover latency requirement (e.g., < 100 ms) and confirm Vehicle module can implement safe-state transition within this window.
Specify network infrastructure SLA (e.g., each path ≥ 0.9999 availability) to support the overall 0.999 target.
Document assumptions about dual-path availability at the worksite.
Define the interface contract for SafetyAlert (link loss triggers safe-state transition).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs to address link detection, redundancy, operator visibility, and network infrastructure. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, but implementation requires detailed design of failover logic, health monitoring, and safe-state transitions. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to allocate these concerns to specific design elements and verify feasibility.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to minimize link unavailability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link status visibility to the tele-operator and site manager.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall support the required bandwidth and latency for teleoperation commands and telemetry.

assumptions: Dual independent network paths (primary + secondary carrier or mesh) are available at the worksite.
Failover latency < 100 ms is acceptable for safe vehicle operation.
Vehicle module will implement automatic safe-state transition on SafetyAlert within [X] ms.
Evaluation period is [X] days/months; 'operational time' excludes scheduled maintenance.
Network infrastructure SLA is negotiated and documented (e.g., each path ≥ 0.9999 availability).
open_risks: Dual-path failure: If both paths fail simultaneously, link availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: Negotiate network SLA; implement automatic safe-state transition.
Operator response time: Operator may not respond to link loss notification in time. Mitigation: Implement automatic safe-state transition; do not rely solely on operator awareness.

Failover latency: If failover latency exceeds safe shutdown window, vehicle may enter unsafe state. Mitigation: Define and test failover latency requirements; implement automatic safe-state transition.
Evaluation period bias: Short evaluation period may not capture rare failure modes. Mitigation: Conduct long-term monitoring; analyze failure modes and trends.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure SLA from primary and secondary carriers (or mesh provider).
Failover latency measurement and safe-state transition timing from Vehicle module.
Operator response time analysis and safe-state transition automation design.
Link availability data from pilot or field trial (minimum [X] days of continuous operation).
Root cause analysis of any link failures during evaluation period.
Verification that dual-path failover logic is correctly implemented and tested.
